death.note: RIT flag lowered to half-staff May 3, 2008 day of his memorial service.

burial.plot_notes: Mount Hope Cemetery, Rochester NY — Section MM Lot 266 (same plot as parents Fred Sr + Florence)

careers.notes: Joined brother Fred Hooker Gordon Jr (HD-prefix above) in 1937. Grew the company from 15 employees to factories in Canada, Australia and England. Awarded the Army-Navy 'E' during WWII for developing technology used to make war products. The company is now a division of SPX Corporation.

Honorary vice chairman. RIT Honorary Doctorate of Humane Letters 1999. Nathaniel Rochester Award. The Gordon Field House and Activities Center named for Lucius + wife Marie 'Tony' (dedicated September 10, 2004). Lucius gave $5 million in gifts and commitments toward the $25M field house. Other named at RIT: Marie and Lucius R. Gordon administrative wing in Student Life Center; Bob Gordon Shell (crew team rowing shell); Lucius Gordon Drive at the RIT Business and Technology Park.

careers.employer: Genesee Hospital, Hillside Children's Center, Allendale Columbia School, Rochester Savings Bank, Industrial Management Council

sources.short: RIT Magazine, 'Remembering Lucius Bob Gordon, RIT Trustee and Benefactor: 1912-2008'
RIT Magazine Fall 2002 — Gordon Field House announcement

notable.notable_blurb: RIT trustee 70-year benefactor; Field House namesake

narrative_blocks.header: 1935 Yukon Gold Rush retraced; 1936 Africa Kodachrome
narrative_blocks.body: Lucius 'Bob' Gordon graduated Phillips Academy Andover (1931) and Yale's Sheffield Scientific School (Industrial Engineering, 1935). After college, he joined his uncle Ward Wickwire on a 1935 trip to the Yukon retracing the 1898 Gold Rush. Then he traveled with his cousin George Cooley Gordon III ( best man at his 1941 wedding) 6,000 miles across Africa from Cairo to Capetown.

narrative_blocks.header: MIXCO grew from 15 employees to global factories
narrative_blocks.body: In 1937, Lucius joined his brother Fred Hooker Gordon Jr. (HD-prefix above) at the Mixing Equipment Company (MIXCO). Together they grew it from 15 employees to factories in Canada, Australia, and England. During WWII the company was awarded the Army-Navy 'E' for developing technology used to make war products — including, with the help of Mechanics Institute (RIT's predecessor), improved aviation fuel for the British and American Air Forces.

narrative_blocks.header: Gordon Field House at RIT dedicated 2004
narrative_blocks.body: Lucius's relationship with RIT spanned seven decades, dating to 1937 when he and his brother Fred reached out to RIT's predecessor Mechanics Institute for machinists, lab technicians, chemists, and co-op students. He joined the RIT Board of Trustees in 1975, served as honorary vice chairman, and received the RIT Honorary Doctorate of Humane Letters in 1999 plus the Nathaniel Rochester Award. By 2002 he sensed RIT lacked one piece of student life — a field house.

cross_connections.evidence: With cousin George C. Gordon III, traveled 6,000 miles Cairo-to-Capetown ~1936. Eastman Kodak commissioned the cousins to test its new Kodachrome film. Lucius served as best man at George III's 1941 wedding (per NYT Aug 7, 1941).
cross_connections.display_label: Cousins traveled Cairo-to-Capetown testing Kodachrome film

Honorary vice chairman. RIT Honorary Doctorate of Humane Letters 1999. Nathaniel Rochester Award. The Gordon Field House and Activities Center named for Lucius + wife Marie 'Tony' (dedicated September 10, 2004). Lucius gave $5 million in gifts and commitments toward the $25M field house. Other named at RIT: Marie and Lucius R. Gordon administrative wing in Student Life Center; Bob Gordon Shell (crew team rowing shell); Lucius Gordon Drive at the RIT Business and Technology Park.

burial.plot_notes: Mount Hope Cemetery, Rochester NY (Section MM Lot 266 with husband Lucius)

marriages.notes: Per EH 5051 + family records: m. Feb 3, 1906 Florence DeMoss Robinson (b.1885 Monroe NY). At least two sons known: Lucius Robinson 'Bob' Gordon (1912-2008, RIT trustee + benefactor with Gordon Field House and Activities Center named for him + RIT honorary doctorate — possible NOTABLE candidate) and a second son not yet identified.

sources.page: p.77 — Whitehall Farms in Clarkson NY

sources.short: RIT 'Remembering Lucius Bob Gordon, RIT Trustee and Benefactor 1912-2008'

narrative_blocks.header: He quit the family bank to farm
narrative_blocks.body: At twenty-nine he walked away from the Gordon family bank for his own ground — Whitehall Farms, north of Brockport. His son Bob spent seventy years giving to a Rochester college that put the family name on its field house.

burial.plot_notes: Mount Hope Cemetery, Rochester NY — Section MM Lot 266
Mount Hope Cemetery, Rochester NY — Section MM Lot 266 (same plot as husband)

death.cause: Hemorrhage of the stomach. Died at his summer residence at Troutburg on Lake Ontario.

burial.plot_notes: Mt. Hope Cemetery, Rochester NY — Lot 61 Range 2 (the Gordon family plot purchased by his father Luther Sr.). Predeceased his wife Ida by ~48 years.

parents.father_research_notes: Luther Gordon Sr. (1818-1881, Brockport bank founder, congressional nominee 1872, lumber merchant)

careers.notes: Succeeded his father Luther Gordon Sr. as bank president 1881; held position until his 1898 death.
With father Luther; family had 7,000+ acres of timberland in Michigan.

sources.page: 'George C. Gordon, the millionaire banker of Brockport, died yesterday at his summer Residence at Troutburg, on the shore of Lake Ontario.'

sources.short: Mt. Hope and Riverside Cemetery Records

sources.page: Reports the 1863 'Bold Bank Robbery' incident: at age 15, working at Waters, Bishop & Co. banking office, George was 'felled by a blow upon the head, dragged into the bank vault and the lock turned upon him' during a $5,000 robbery.

notable.primary_url_label: NYT obituary August 26, 1898 (no current online URL)

narrative_blocks.body: George C. Gordon was already at work in his father's banking world when, at age 15 on July 23, 1863, he was attending the banking office of Waters, Bishop & Co. A man asked him to change a ten-dollar bill. Per the Daily Union and Advertiser: 'he was felled by a blow upon the head, dragged into the bank vault and the lock turned upon him.' He was found 'severely beaten upon the head, and was bleeding from a wound,' and $5,000 had been stolen.

narrative_blocks.header: Succeeded father as Brockport bank president
narrative_blocks.body: Upon his father Luther's 1881 death, George C. Gordon stepped into the presidency of the First National Bank of Brockport — the bank his father had founded as the repository for Erie Canal tolls collected at Brockport. He held that position until his own 1898 death.

bio.extra_names: Per Mt. Hope Cemetery + Gordon family records: also recorded as 'Ida Mae'

marriages.notes: Per EH 3892: m. Nov 18, 1873 George Cooley Gordon (b. July 1, 1849 Rushford NY, son of Luther + Florilla Cooley Gordon; d. Aug 25, 1898). FOUR sons per EH 5049-5052: Luther Gordon (b.1875, m.1904 Augusta Moore, 2 children Luther + Emeline), George Cooley Gordon (b.1877, m.1906 Cecelia Mitchell Kimball, dau Cecelia), Fred Hooker Gordon (b.1881, m.1906 Florence De Moss Robinson), Thomas Cullen Gordon (b.1884, m.1908 Ruth Little Perry). Two of her sons named for Hooker family: Fred HOOKER Gordon and Thomas CULLEN Gordon — `named_for_hooker` pattern applies to those two.

narrative_blocks.header: Brockport's banker matriarch served Rochester children
narrative_blocks.body: Ida May Hooker — born January 1855 in Kendall NY, daughter of Thomas Cullen Hooker — moved to Brockport NY when her father relocated the family there. She married George Cooley Gordon Sr. on October 18, 1875 when she was 20. George went on to become president of the First National Bank of Brockport, a 'millionaire banker' per his New York Times obituary.

narrative_blocks.header: Gordon Hall and Tiffany window honor her
narrative_blocks.body: Ida and George's enduring presence in Brockport survived her death by decades. In 1927, a Tiffany window made by Louis C. Tiffany was installed at St. Luke's Episcopal Church in Brockport, dedicated to George and Ida's memory.

narrative_blocks.header: Among first women on SUNY Brockport Board, 1917
narrative_blocks.body: In 1917 Ida May Hooker Gordon was appointed to the Board of Trustees of SUNY Brockport, one of the first two women to serve on the board. The appointment paralleled her presidency of the Rochester Children's Nursery — extending her institutional leadership beyond philanthropy into educational governance. She continued to serve into her later years before her death at 91.

burial.plot_notes: Mt. Hope Cemetery, Rochester NY — Lot 61 Range 2 — purchased by her father-in-law Luther Gordon Sr. Plot features a stunning monument with columns and an allegorical figure with drapery. George C. Gordon her husband paid $9,229.54 for perpetual care in 1883.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
